### Fabrication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Fabrication")]

Nvidia [uses external suppliers for all phases of manufacturing](/wiki/Fabless_manufacturing "Fabless manufacturing"), including wafer fabrication, assembly, testing, and packaging. Nvidia thus avoids most of the investment and production costs and risks associated with chip manufacturing, although it does sometimes directly procure some components and materials used in the production of its products (e.g., memory and substrates). Nvidia focuses its own resources on product design, quality assurance, marketing, and customer support.[[188]](#cite_note-annualreview2-189)[[189]](#cite_note-valuechain2-190)

## GPU Technology Conference

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: GPU Technology Conference")]

Main article: [Nvidia GTC](/wiki/Nvidia_GTC "Nvidia GTC")

Nvidia's GPU Technology Conference (GTC) is a series of technical conferences held around the world.[[190]](#cite_note-GPU_Technology_Conference-191) It originated in 2009 in San Jose, California, with an initial focus on the potential for solving computing challenges through GPUs.[[24]](#cite_note-timeline-25) In recent years,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the conference's focus has shifted to various applications of artificial intelligence and [deep learning](/wiki/Deep_learning "Deep learning"); including [self-driving cars](/wiki/Autonomous_car "Autonomous car"), healthcare, [high-performance computing](/wiki/High_Performance_Computing "High Performance Computing"), and Nvidia Deep Learning Institute (DLI) training.[[191]](#cite_note-192) GTC 2018 attracted over 8400 attendees.[[190]](#cite_note-GPU_Technology_Conference-191) GTC 2020 was converted to a digital event and drew roughly 59,000 registrants.[[192]](#cite_note-193) After several years of remote-only events, GTC in March 2024 returned to an in-person format in San Jose, California.[[193]](#cite_note-194)

At GTC 2025, Nvidia unveiled its next-generation AI hardware, the Blackwell Ultra and Vera Rubin chips, signaling a leap toward [agentic AI](/wiki/Agentic_AI "Agentic AI") and [reasoning-capable computing](/wiki/Reasoning_AI "Reasoning AI"). Huang projected that AI-driven infrastructure would drive Nvidia's data center revenue to $1 trillion by 2028. The announcement also introduced Isaac GR00T N1 (humanoid robotics model), Cosmos ([synthetic training data](/wiki/Synthetic_training_data "Synthetic training data") AI), and the Newton physics engine, developed in collaboration with DeepMind and Disney Research.[[194]](#cite_note-195)

## Product families

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Product families")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/NVIDIA_Shield_Tablet_with_Wireless_Controller_%2816131034479%29.jpg/250px-NVIDIA_Shield_Tablet_with_Wireless_Controller_%2816131034479%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:NVIDIA_Shield_Tablet_with_Wireless_Controller_(16131034479).jpg)

A [Shield Tablet](/wiki/Shield_Tablet "Shield Tablet") with its accompanying input pen (left) and [gamepad](/wiki/Gamepad "Gamepad")

Nvidia's product families include graphics processing units, wireless communication devices, and automotive hardware and software, such as:

* [GeForce](/wiki/GeForce "GeForce"), consumer-oriented graphics processing products
* [RTX](/wiki/Nvidia_RTX "Nvidia RTX"), professional visual computing graphics processing products (replacing GTX and [Quadro](/wiki/Quadro "Quadro"))
* [NVS](/wiki/Nvidia_Quadro#For_business_NVS "Nvidia Quadro"), a multi-display business graphics processor
* [Tegra](/wiki/Tegra "Tegra"), a [system on a chip](/wiki/System_on_a_chip "System on a chip") series for mobile devices
* [Tesla](/wiki/Nvidia_Tesla "Nvidia Tesla"), line of dedicated general-purpose GPUs for high-end image generation applications in professional and scientific fields
* [nForce](/wiki/NForce "NForce"), a motherboard chipset created by Nvidia for Intel (Celeron, Pentium and Core 2) and AMD (Athlon and Duron) microprocessors
* [GRID](/wiki/Nvidia_GRID "Nvidia GRID"), a set of hardware and services by Nvidia for graphics virtualization
* Shield, a range of gaming hardware including the [Shield Portable](/wiki/Shield_Portable "Shield Portable"), [Shield Tablet](/wiki/Shield_Tablet "Shield Tablet") and [Shield TV](/wiki/Shield_TV "Shield TV")
* [Drive](/wiki/Nvidia_Drive "Nvidia Drive"), a range of hardware and software products for designers and manufacturers of autonomous vehicles. The [Drive PX-series](/wiki/Drive_PX-series "Drive PX-series") is a high-performance computer platform aimed at autonomous driving through deep learning,[[195]](#cite_note-196) while Driveworks is an operating system for [driverless cars](/wiki/Self-driving_car "Self-driving car").[[196]](#cite_note-197)
* [BlueField](/wiki/Nvidia_BlueField "Nvidia BlueField"), a range of [data processing units](/wiki/Data_processing_unit "Data processing unit"), initially inherited from their acquisition of [Mellanox Technologies](/wiki/Mellanox_Technologies "Mellanox Technologies")[[197]](#cite_note-198)[[198]](#cite_note-199)
* Datacenter/server class CPU, codenamed Grace, released in 2023[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-201)
* [DGX](/wiki/Nvidia_DGX "Nvidia DGX"), an enterprise platform designed for deep learning applications
* Maxine, a platform providing developers a suite of AI-based conferencing software[[201]](#cite_note-202)
* Omniverse, a platform for creating and operating [metaverse](/wiki/Metaverse "Metaverse") applications[[202]](#cite_note-203)

## Open-source software support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Open-source software support")]

See also: [Free and open-source graphics device driver](/wiki/Free_and_open-source_graphics_device_driver "Free and open-source graphics device driver"), [Mesa 3D](/wiki/Mesa_3D "Mesa 3D"), and [OpenPOWER Foundation](/wiki/OpenPOWER_Foundation "OpenPOWER Foundation")

Until September 23, 2013, Nvidia had not published any documentation for its advanced hardware,[[203]](#cite_note-204) meaning that programmers could not write [free and open-source](/wiki/Free_and_open-source "Free and open-source") [device drivers](/wiki/Device_driver "Device driver") for its products without resorting to [reverse engineering](/wiki/Reverse_engineering "Reverse engineering"). Additionally, features like their compute platform [CUDA](/wiki/CUDA "CUDA") and the [DLSS](/wiki/DLSS "DLSS") technology suite are proprietary and only available on their hardware. As such, Nvidia has been notable for proprietization and vendor-locking practices.

Nvidia has released Linux  under dual GPL/MIT licensing, allowing developers to inspect the driver module code and contribute improvements in collaboration with the community.[[1]](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/nvidia-releases-open-source-gpu-kernel-modules) The Nvidia  on GitHub provides the publicly accessible source code for these modules, supporting modern GPU architectures in Linux environments.[[204]](#cite_note-github-205) Furthermore, Nvidia’s acquisition of  and the release of new open-source AI models highlight its broader commitment to open-source software and the AI ecosystem [[2]](https://www.reuters.com/business/nvidia-buys-ai-software-provider-schedmd-expand-open-source-ai-push-2025-12-15/)

Instead, Nvidia provides its own binary GeForce graphics drivers for [X.Org](/wiki/X.Org_Server "X.Org Server") and an open-source library that interfaces with the [Linux](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel"), [FreeBSD](/wiki/FreeBSD "FreeBSD") or [Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(operating_system) "Solaris (operating system)") kernels and the proprietary graphics software. Nvidia also provided but stopped supporting an obfuscated open-source driver that only supports two-dimensional hardware acceleration and ships with the X.Org distribution.[[205]](#cite_note-206)

The proprietary nature of Nvidia's drivers has generated dissatisfaction within [free-software communities](/wiki/Free_software_community "Free software community"). In a 2012 talk, [Linus Torvalds](/wiki/Linus_Torvalds "Linus Torvalds") gave a middle-finger gesture and criticized Nvidia’s stance toward Linux.[[206]](#cite_note-207)[[207]](#cite_note-208) Some Linux and BSD users insist on using only open-source drivers and regard Nvidia's insistence on providing nothing more than a binary-only driver as inadequate, given that competing manufacturers such as [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") offer support and documentation for [open-source](/wiki/Open-source_model "Open-source model") developers, and others like [AMD](/wiki/Advanced_Micro_Devices "Advanced Micro Devices") release partial documentation and provide some active development.[[208]](#cite_note-209)[[209]](#cite_note-ubuntugamer-210)

Nvidia only provides x86/x64 and ARMv7-A versions of their proprietary driver; as a result, features like [CUDA](/wiki/CUDA "CUDA") are unavailable on other platforms.[[210]](#cite_note-211) Some users claim that Nvidia's Linux drivers impose artificial restrictions, like limiting the number of monitors that can be used at the same time, but the company has not commented on these accusations.[[211]](#cite_note-212)

In 2014, with its Maxwell GPUs, Nvidia started to require firmware by them to unlock all features of its graphics cards.[[212]](#cite_note-213)[[213]](#cite_note-214)[[214]](#cite_note-215)

On May 12, 2022, Nvidia announced that they are opensourcing their GPU kernel modules.[[215]](#cite_note-216)[[216]](#cite_note-217)[[204]](#cite_note-github-205) Support for Nvidia's firmware was implemented in nouveau in 2023, which allows proper power management and GPU reclocking for Turing and newer graphics card generations.[[217]](#cite_note-218)[[218]](#cite_note-219)

In July 21, 2025, Nvidia announced to extend CUDA support to RISC-V.[[219]](#cite_note-220)[[220]](#cite_note-221)[[221]](#cite_note-222)

## Deep learning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Deep learning")]

Nvidia GPUs are used in [deep learning](/wiki/Deep_learning "Deep learning"), and accelerated analytics due to Nvidia's [CUDA](/wiki/CUDA "CUDA") software platform and API which allows programmers to utilize the higher number of cores present in GPUs to [parallelize](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") [BLAS](/wiki/BLAS "BLAS") operations which are extensively used in [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") algorithms.[[11]](#cite_note-Elsevier-12) They were included in many Tesla, Inc. vehicles before Musk announced at Tesla Autonomy Day in 2019 that the company developed its own SoC and full self-driving computer now and would stop using Nvidia hardware for their vehicles.[[223]](#cite_note-224)[[224]](#cite_note-225) These GPUs are used by researchers, laboratories, tech companies and enterprise companies.[[225]](#cite_note-226) In 2009, Nvidia was involved in what was called the "big bang" of deep learning, "as deep-learning neural networks were combined with Nvidia graphics processing units (GPUs)".[[226]](#cite_note-227) That year, the [Google Brain](/wiki/Google_Brain "Google Brain") team used Nvidia GPUs to create [deep neural networks](/wiki/Deep_neural_networks "Deep neural networks") capable of machine learning, where [Andrew Ng](/wiki/Andrew_Ng "Andrew Ng") determined that GPUs could increase the speed of deep learning systems by about 100 times.[[227]](#cite_note-228)

### DGX

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: DGX")]

Main article: [Nvidia DGX](/wiki/Nvidia_DGX "Nvidia DGX")

DGX is a line of [supercomputers](/wiki/Supercomputers "Supercomputers") by Nvidia.

In April 2016, Nvidia produced the [DGX-1](/wiki/DGX-1 "DGX-1") based on an 8 GPU cluster, to improve the ability of users to use deep learning by combining GPUs with integrated deep learning software.[[228]](#cite_note-229) Nvidia gifted its first DGX-1 to [OpenAI](/wiki/OpenAI "OpenAI") in August 2016 to help it train larger and more complex AI models with the capability of reducing processing time from six days to two hours.[[229]](#cite_note-230)[[230]](#cite_note-231) It also developed Nvidia Tesla K80 and P100 GPU-based virtual machines, which are available through [Google Cloud](/wiki/Google_Cloud_Platform "Google Cloud Platform"), which Google installed in November 2016.[[231]](#cite_note-232) [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") added GPU servers in a preview offering of its N series based on Nvidia's Tesla K80s, each containing 4992 processing cores. Later that year, AWS's P2 instance was produced using up to 16 Nvidia Tesla K80 GPUs. That month Nvidia also partnered with IBM to create a software kit that boosts the AI capabilities of [Watson](/wiki/Watson_(computer) "Watson (computer)"),[[232]](#cite_note-233) called IBM PowerAI.[[233]](#cite_note-234)[[234]](#cite_note-235) Nvidia also offers its own Nvidia Deep Learning software development kit.[[235]](#cite_note-236) In 2017, the GPUs were also brought online at the [Riken](/wiki/Riken "Riken") Center for Advanced Intelligence Project for [Fujitsu](/wiki/Fujitsu "Fujitsu").[[236]](#cite_note-237) The company's deep learning technology led to a boost in its 2017 earnings.[[237]](#cite_note-238)

In 2018, Nvidia researchers demonstrated imitation-learning techniques for industrial robots. They have created a system that, after a short revision and testing, can already be used to control the universal robots of the next generation. In addition to GPU manufacturing, Nvidia provides parallel processing capabilities to researchers and scientists that allow them to efficiently run high-performance applications.[[238]](#cite_note-239)

### Robotics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Robotics")]

In 2020, Nvidia unveiled "[Omniverse](/wiki/Nvidia_Omniverse "Nvidia Omniverse")", a virtual environment designed for engineers.[[239]](#cite_note-240) Nvidia also open-sourced Isaac Sim, which makes use of this Omniverse to train robots through simulations that mimic the physics of the robots and the real world.[[240]](#cite_note-241)[[241]](#cite_note-242)

In 2024, Huang oriented Nvidia's focus towards [humanoid robots](/wiki/Humanoid_robot "Humanoid robot") and [self-driving cars](/wiki/Self-driving_car "Self-driving car"), which he expects to gain widespread adoption.[[242]](#cite_note-243)[[243]](#cite_note-244)

In 2025, Nvidia announced Isaac GR00T N1, an open-source [foundation model](/wiki/Foundation_model "Foundation model") "designed to expedite the development and capabilities of humanoid robots". [Neura Robotics](/wiki/Neura_Robotics "Neura Robotics"), [1X Technologies](/wiki/1X_Technologies "1X Technologies") and [Vention](/wiki/Vention "Vention") are among the first companies to use the model.[[244]](#cite_note-245)[[245]](#cite_note-246)[[246]](#cite_note-247)

## Inception Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Inception Program")]

Nvidia's Inception Program was created to support startups making exceptional advances in the fields of artificial intelligence and data science. Award winners are announced at Nvidia's GTC Conference. In May 2017, the program had 1,300 companies.[[247]](#cite_note-NVI15-248) As of March 2018, there were 2,800 startups in the Inception Program.[[248]](#cite_note-249) As of August 2021, the program has over 8,500 members in 90 countries, with cumulative funding of US$60 billion.[[249]](#cite_note-250)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### GTX 970 hardware specifications advertising dispute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: GTX 970 hardware specifications advertising dispute")]

Main article: [GeForce 900 series § Advertising controversy](/wiki/GeForce_900_series#Advertising_controversy "GeForce 900 series")

Issues with the GeForce GTX 970's specifications were first brought up by users when they found out that the cards, while featuring 4 GB of memory, rarely accessed memory over the 3.5 GB boundary. Further testing and investigation eventually led to Nvidia issuing a statement that the card's initially announced specifications had been altered without notice before the card was made commercially available, and that the card took a performance hit once memory over the 3.5 GB limit were put into use.[[250]](#cite_note-251)[[251]](#cite_note-252)[[252]](#cite_note-253)

The card's back-end hardware specifications, initially announced as being identical to those of the GeForce GTX 980, differed in the amount of L2 cache (1.75 MB versus 2 MB in the GeForce GTX 980) and the number of ROPs (56 versus 64 in the 980). Additionally, it was revealed that the card was designed to access its memory as a 3.5 GB section, plus a 0.5 GB one, access to the latter being 7 times slower than the first one.[[253]](#cite_note-254) The company then went on to promise a specific driver modification to alleviate the performance issues produced by the cutbacks suffered by the card.[[254]](#cite_note-255) However, Nvidia later clarified that the promise had been a miscommunication and there would be no specific driver update for the GTX 970.[[255]](#cite_note-256) Nvidia claimed that it would assist customers who wanted refunds in obtaining them.[[256]](#cite_note-257) On February 26, 2015, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang went on record in Nvidia's official blog to apologize for the incident.[[257]](#cite_note-258) In February 2015 a class-action lawsuit alleging false advertising was filed against Nvidia and Gigabyte Technology in the [United States District Court for the Northern District of California](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_California "United States District Court for the Northern District of California").[[258]](#cite_note-970lawsuit-259)[[259]](#cite_note-260)

Nvidia revealed that it is able to disable individual units, each containing 256 KB of L2 cache and 8 ROPs, without disabling whole memory controllers.[[260]](#cite_note-AnandTechCorrectionPage2-261) This comes at the cost of dividing the memory bus into high speed and low speed segments that cannot be accessed at the same time unless one segment is reading while the other segment is writing because the L2/ROP unit managing both of the GDDR5 controllers shares the read return channel and the write data bus between the two GDDR5 controllers and itself.[[260]](#cite_note-AnandTechCorrectionPage2-261) This is used in the GeForce GTX 970, which therefore can be described as having 3.5 GB in its high speed segment on a 224-bit bus and 0.5 GB in a low speed segment on a 32-bit bus.[[260]](#cite_note-AnandTechCorrectionPage2-261)

On July 27, 2016, Nvidia agreed to a preliminary settlement of the U.S. class action lawsuit,[[258]](#cite_note-970lawsuit-259) offering a $30 refund on GTX 970 purchases. The agreed upon refund represents the portion of the cost of the storage and performance capabilities the consumers assumed they were obtaining when they purchased the card.[[261]](#cite_note-262)

### GeForce Partner Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: GeForce Partner Program")]

Main article: [GeForce Partner Program](/wiki/GeForce_Partner_Program "GeForce Partner Program")

The Nvidia GeForce Partner Program was a [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing") program designed to provide partnering companies with benefits such as public relations support, [video game bundling](/wiki/Video_game_bundle "Video game bundle"), and marketing development funds.[[262]](#cite_note-263) The program proved to be controversial, with complaints about it possibly being an [anti-competitive](/wiki/Anti-competitive "Anti-competitive") practice.[[263]](#cite_note-:02-264)

First announced in a blog post on March 1, 2018,[[264]](#cite_note-265) it was canceled on May 4, 2018.[[265]](#cite_note-266)

### Hardware Unboxed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Hardware Unboxed")]

On December 10, 2020, Nvidia told [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") tech reviewer Steven Walton of Hardware Unboxed that it would no longer supply him with GeForce Founders Edition graphics card review units.[[266]](#cite_note-267)[[267]](#cite_note-268) In a Twitter message, Hardware Unboxed said, "Nvidia have officially decided to ban us from receiving GeForce Founders Edition GPU review samples. Their reasoning is that we are focusing on rasterization instead of ray tracing. They have said they will revisit this 'should your editorial direction change.'"[[268]](#cite_note-269)

In emails that were disclosed by Walton from Nvidia Senior PR Manager Bryan Del Rizzo, Nvidia had said:

> ...your GPU reviews and recommendations have continued to focus singularly on rasterization performance, and you have largely discounted all of the other technologies we offer gamers. It is very clear from your community commentary that you do not see things the same way that we, gamers, and the rest of the industry do.[[269]](#cite_note-:1-270)

TechSpot, partner site of Hardware Unboxed, said, "this and other related incidents raise serious questions around journalistic independence and what they are expecting of reviewers when they are sent products for an unbiased opinion."[[269]](#cite_note-:1-270)

A number of technology reviewers came out strongly against Nvidia's move.[[270]](#cite_note-:2-271)[[271]](#cite_note-272) [Linus Sebastian](/wiki/Linus_Sebastian "Linus Sebastian"), of [Linus Tech Tips](/wiki/Linus_Tech_Tips "Linus Tech Tips"), titled the episode of his weekly WAN Show, "NVIDIA might ACTUALLY be EVIL..."[[272]](#cite_note-273) and was highly critical of the company's move to dictate specific outcomes of technology reviews.[[273]](#cite_note-274) The review site Gamers Nexus said it was, "Nvidia's latest decision to shoot both its feet: They've now made it so that any reviewers covering RT will become subject to scrutiny from untrusting viewers who will suspect subversion by the company. Shortsighted self-own from NVIDIA."[[274]](#cite_note-275)

Two days later, Nvidia reversed their stance.[[275]](#cite_note-276)[[276]](#cite_note-277) Hardware Unboxed sent out a Twitter message, "I just received an email from Nvidia apologizing for the previous email & they've now walked everything back."[[277]](#cite_note-278)[[270]](#cite_note-:2-271) On December 14, Hardware Unboxed released a video explaining the controversy from their viewpoint.[[278]](#cite_note-279) Via Twitter, they also shared a second apology sent by Nvidia's Del Rizzo that said "to withhold samples because I didn't agree with your commentary is simply inexcusable and crossed the line."[[279]](#cite_note-280)[[280]](#cite_note-281)

### Improper disclosures about cryptomining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Improper disclosures about cryptomining")]

In 2018, Nvidia's chips became popular for [cryptomining](/wiki/Cryptomining "Cryptomining"), the process of obtaining crypto rewards in exchange for verifying transactions on distributed ledgers, the [United States Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/United_States_Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "United States Securities and Exchange Commission") (SEC) said. However, the company failed to disclose that it was a "significant element" of its revenue growth from sales of chips designed for gaming, the SEC further added in a statement and charging order. Those omissions misled investors and analysts who were interested in understanding the impact of cryptomining on Nvidia's business, the SEC emphasized. Nvidia, which did not admit or deny the findings, has agreed to pay $5.5 million to settle civil charges, according to a statement made by the SEC in May 2022.[[281]](#cite_note-282)

### French Competition Authority Investigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: French Competition Authority Investigation")]

On September 26, 2023, Nvidia's French offices were searched by the French Competition Authority. The raid, authorized by a judge, was part of an investigation into suspected anti-competitive practices in the graphics card sector. Nvidia has not publicly commented on the incident.[[282]](#cite_note-283)

### AI regulation dispute with Anthropic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: AI regulation dispute with Anthropic")]

In July 2025, a public dispute emerged between Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang and [Anthropic](/wiki/Anthropic "Anthropic") CEO [Dario Amodei](/wiki/Dario_Amodei "Dario Amodei") over AI regulation and industry practices. The conflict escalated when Amodei vehemently denied Huang's allegations that he sought to control the AI industry through safety concerns, calling Huang's claims an "outrageous lie."[[283]](#cite_note-fortune2025-284) The dispute centered on differing philosophies regarding AI development, with Amodei advocating for stronger regulatory oversight and "responsible scaling policies", while Huang promoted open-source development and criticized what Nvidia characterized as "regulatory capture".[[283]](#cite_note-fortune2025-284) Nvidia responded by stating that "lobbying for regulatory capture against open source will only stifle innovation, make AI less safe and secure, and less democratic."[[283]](#cite_note-fortune2025-284) The controversy highlighted broader tensions within the AI industry between companies favoring rapid development and those emphasizing safety measures and regulation.[[283]](#cite_note-fortune2025-284)

### Proposed Shanghai facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Proposed Shanghai facility")]

In May 2025, U.S. senators [Jim Banks](/wiki/Jim_Banks "Jim Banks") and [Elizabeth Warren](/wiki/Elizabeth_Warren "Elizabeth Warren") criticized a proposed Nvidia facility in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), saying that it "raises significant national security and economic security issues that warrant serious review."[[284]](#cite_note-285)

### Chinese market halt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Chinese market halt")]

In August 2025, Nvidia ordered suppliers to halt production of its H20 AI chip following Chinese government directives warning domestic companies against purchasing the processor due to security concerns.[[285]](#cite_note-reuters-h20-halt-286)[[286]](#cite_note-info-h20-halt-287) The company directed suppliers including [Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Taiwan_Semiconductor_Manufacturing_Company "Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company"), Samsung Electronics, and [Amkor Technology](/wiki/Amkor_Technology "Amkor Technology") to suspend work on the China-focused processor.[[287]](#cite_note-yahoo-h20-suppliers-288)

The H20 was developed in late 2023 specifically for the Chinese market to comply with U.S. export restrictions, featuring 96GB of [HBM3](/wiki/HBM3 "HBM3") memory and 4.0 TB/s memory bandwidth—higher than the H100—but with significantly reduced computational power at 296 [TFLOPs](/wiki/FLOPS "FLOPS") compared to the H100's 1979 TFLOPs.[[288]](#cite_note-futunn-h20-specs-289)[[289]](#cite_note-binance-h20-performance-290) Despite lower raw performance, the H20 demonstrated over 20% faster performance than the H100 in [large language model](/wiki/Large_language_model "Large language model") inference tasks due to architectural optimizations.[[288]](#cite_note-futunn-h20-specs-289)[[289]](#cite_note-binance-h20-performance-290)

Prior to the production halt, Nvidia had placed substantial orders for the H20, including 300,000 units from TSMC in July 2025, driven by strong demand from Chinese technology companies.[[151]](#cite_note-Reuters-152) CEO [Jensen Huang](/wiki/Jensen_Huang "Jensen Huang") denied allegations that the H20 contained security backdoors, stating the chips were designed solely for commercial use.[[290]](#cite_note-ainvest-huang-backdoors-291) The production suspension occurred as Nvidia was developing the B30A, a new chip based on its Blackwell architecture intended to succeed the H20 in the Chinese market.[[291]](#cite_note-reuters-b30a-chip-292)

Following prolonged negotiations, the United States announced in 2026 that it would permit the export of newer H200 chips to China under specified conditions. The move was expected to stimulate market activity. The Chinese market, estimated to be worth hundreds of billions and potentially trillions of U.S. dollars, presents significant profit opportunities for American firms, with revenues reinforcing U.S. leading positions.[[292]](#cite_note-293)[[293]](#cite_note-294)

The decision also drew criticism for simultaneously constraining and strengthening China’s technological capabilities, and was described by some critics as unreasonable.[[294]](#cite_note-295) However, some technology experts argue that China’s development capacity has exceeded expectations and warn that the full ban policy could potentially enable real challenges to U.S. influence over technical standard setting.[[295]](#cite_note-296)[[296]](#cite_note-297) China also quickly responded by imposing restrictions on certain foreign chips to be imported.[[297]](#cite_note-298) Despite China’s efforts to shape technical standards, still, American products and ecosystem retain strong appeal, raising government concerns over possible fragmentation and reduced reinvestment in their own artificial intelligence progress.[[298]](#cite_note-299) More broadly, China is seen as seeking long-term influence to counterbalance the United States rather than maintaining competitive complementarity rival.[[299]](#cite_note-300)

### Data scraping & Anna's Archive piracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Data scraping & Anna's Archive piracy")]

See also: [Anna's Archive § Nvidia lawsuit](/wiki/Anna%27s_Archive#Nvidia_lawsuit "Anna's Archive")

Nvidia has been involved in heavy data scraping for illegal AI model training. In 2024, internal conversations revealed that Nvidia was scraping large amounts of videos from YouTube without permission, and by circumventing protection measures.[[300]](#cite_note-301) In January 2026, a court filing revealed that Nvidia developers contacted shadow library [Anna's Archive](/wiki/Anna%27s_Archive "Anna's Archive") to evaluate the use of pirated content for model training, which management gave green light for despite legality concerns from the site.[[301]](#cite_note-302) Nvidia denied the allegations and filed a motion to dismiss the lawsuit.[[302]](#cite_note-303)[[303]](#cite_note-304)[[304]](#cite_note-305)

### Deepseek and Nvidia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nvidia&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Deepseek and Nvidia")]

In February 2026, it was announced by the Trump Administration that Deepseek's latest AI model which was trained on Nvidia's most advanced AI chip would be released as soon as March 2026. This represented a potential violation of US export controls.[[305]](#cite_note-306)
